name: Refined Type I Multi-Panel Codebook

lineage.adds: each panel picks its own beam direction, on very large aggregated multi-panel arrays

tagline: Multi-panel Type I, scaled up — but now every panel points its own spotlight instead of sharing one direction across the whole array.
overview: Release 15's multi-panel Type I (also in this catalog) shares a single beam direction across every panel and only reports how to phase-align the panels afterward — like several spotlights all aimed the same way, adjusted only for cable-length differences. Refined Type I Multi-Panel, targeting Release 19's very large **aggregated** arrays (up to 128 ports assembled from multiple panels), goes further: **each panel selects its own beam direction**, independently, over the full oversampled grid — plus its own companion-beam offset at ranks 2-4. Different panels can genuinely point at different angles.

The supported geometries are the Release-19 multi-panel table: combinations like 2 panels of 4x3 ports (48 total), 2 panels of 8x2 (64 total), or 4 panels of 8x2 (128 total) — always with the fixed oversampling $(O_1,O_2)=(4,4)$ that all the large-array Release-19 codebooks share. On top of each panel's independent beam and offset, the codebook keeps the R15 multi-panel machinery for aligning the panels: wideband **inter-panel co-phasing** indices, and per-subband, per-panel polarization co-phasing.

Conceptually this is the natural generalization: single-panel Type I got "one direction gets independent companions at high rank" (the sibling refined single-panel codebook); multi-panel Type I gets "every panel gets its own direction." Both refinements answer the same underlying observation — that a single shared parameter (one beam, or one fixed offset table) becomes an increasingly poor approximation as the array grows large and structurally complex.

As with the other Release-19 refined codebooks in this family, the reconstruction machinery — DFT (discrete Fourier transform) beams, the QPSK (quadrature phase-shift keying) co-phasing families, the panel-major port layout — is inherited essentially unchanged from R15 multi-panel Type I. What changes is purely the *configuration and selection freedom*: each panel now searches and reports its own beam rather than sharing one across the array.

howItWorks.title: UE measures the channel across all panels of a large aggregated array
howItWorks.body: The phone estimates the channel via CSI-RS (channel state information reference signal) across an array built from several panels totaling 48, 64, or 128 ports.

howItWorks.title: UE selects a beam independently for each panel
howItWorks.body: Unlike R15 multi-panel (one shared beam), each panel's own best direction is found separately on the oversampled grid, plus, at ranks 2-4, that panel's own companion-beam offset.

howItWorks.title: UE finds inter-panel co-phasing
howItWorks.body: Just as in R15 multi-panel, the UE reports wideband QPSK phase offsets aligning each panel to the reference panel.

howItWorks.title: UE refines per-subband, per-panel co-phasing
howItWorks.body: For each frequency unit, per-panel polarization co-phasing is chosen to maximize the reconstructed precoder's quality.

howItWorks.title: Report travels over the uplink
howItWorks.body: Every panel's independent beam and offset indices, the inter-panel phase offsets, and the per-subband co-phasing form the PMI (precoding matrix indicator).

howItWorks.title: gNB reconstructs and transmits
howItWorks.body: The base station (gNB) rebuilds each panel's own beam, applies the inter-panel and per-subband phase corrections, and transmits across the full aggregated array.

whatIsReported.plain: Each panel's own horizontal beam position (reported per panel).
whatIsReported.detail: Unlike R15 multi-panel's single shared i11, this codebook reports one horizontal beam index per panel over the large oversampled grid.

whatIsReported.plain: Each panel's own vertical beam position.
whatIsReported.detail: Paired with i11, one per panel.

whatIsReported.plain: Each panel's own companion-beam offset selector (ranks 2-4).
whatIsReported.detail: The same fixed-offset table style as the refined single-panel codebook's low-rank case, applied independently per panel.

whatIsReported.plain: Wideband inter-panel co-phasing, aligning each panel to the reference panel.
whatIsReported.detail: Same role as R15 multi-panel's i14: one QPSK phase index per non-reference panel.
Refreshed every frequency unit, same structural role as R15 multi-panel's i2.

parametersExplained.plain: Not applicable to this codebook (that parameter belongs to the CJT family) — the relevant geometry knob here is the panel count Ng, chosen together with N1/N2 from the Release-19 multi-panel table.
parametersExplained.guidance: Pick one of the supported (Ng, N1, N2) combinations for the target port count (48, 64, or 128).

parametersExplained.plain: How many simultaneous data streams (1-4) to describe.
parametersExplained.guidance: Multi-panel codebooks in this family cap at rank 4, reflecting the added complexity of independently coordinating multiple panels.

strengths: Each panel points its own direction, closing the biggest structural gap in R15 multi-panel's shared-beam design.
Scales cleanly to the large Release-19 aggregated arrays (up to 128 ports).
Reuses proven R15 multi-panel co-phasing and reconstruction machinery.
Still far cheaper than any Type II-family report.
limitations: Only defined for the fixed set of Release-19 multi-panel geometries.
Independent per-panel beams cost more bits than R15's single shared beam.
Capped at rank 4, same as R15 multi-panel.
Like all Type I variants, reports directions rather than weighted combinations, so it remains coarser than Type II on richly scattered channels.
whenToUse: Use this codebook to model very large antenna installations physically assembled from multiple independently-steerable panels, where each panel may need to point at a genuinely different direction.

mathHighlight.caption: Each panel contributes its own beam vector to the stacked precoder column, phase-aligned to the reference panel by the inter-panel co-phasing terms.
mathHighlight.latex: W^{(1)} \propto \big[\, v^{(0)};\ \varphi_n v^{(0)};\ a_1 v^{(1)};\ \varphi_n a_1 v^{(1)};\ \dots \,\big]^{\top}
